pattern: A mountain array is two monotone runs glued at one peak, so locating the peak with binary search first turns the whole lookup into two more binary searches, one per slope.
hints: Scanning left to right with .get() works but can burn the call budget on a long array before you even reach the target.
Binary search for the peak by comparing an index to its neighbor: climbing means the peak is further right, falling means it is at or before you.

Once you have the peak, binary search the ascending prefix for the target, and only search the descending suffix if that first search comes up empty — this also guarantees you report the smallest matching index when the value occurs on both slopes.

edge_inputs.description: target value occurs on both slopes; the answer must be the smaller (ascending-side) index
minimal length-3 mountain, target at the very first index
minimal length-3 mountain, target at the very last index
target is not present anywhere in the array
target lives only on the ascending slope
target lives only on the descending slope, well past the peak
a wider mountain to exercise all three binary searches inside the get() call budget, target unique to the descending slope

stress.description: random mountain shape with a random target that may or may not be present
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(3, min(size, 5000))
    peak = rng.randint(1, n - 2)
    left_n = peak + 1
    right_n = n - peak
    base = rng.randint(-1000, 1000)
    asc = [base + i * rng.randint(1, 5) for i in range(left_n)]
    desc = []
    prev = asc[-1]
    for _ in range(right_n):
        prev -= rng.randint(1, 5)
        desc.append(prev)
    arr = asc + desc
    if rng.random() < 0.5:
        target = rng.choice(arr)
    else:
        target = min(arr) - rng.randint(1, 100)
    return [target, {'arr': arr, 'budget': 100}]
